title: Adding Powers
description: Suppose you are performing the following algorithm. There is an array $$$v_1, v_2, \dots, v_n$$$ filled with zeroes at start. The following operation is applied to the array several times — at $$$i$$$-th step ($$$0$$$-indexed) you can: either choose position $$$pos$$$ ($$$1 \le pos \le n$$$) and increase $$$v_{pos}$$$ by $$$k^i$$$; or not choose any position and skip this step. You can choose how the algorithm would behave on each step and when to stop it. The question is: can you make array $$$v$$$ equal to the given array $$$a$$$ ($$$v_j = a_j$$$ for each $$$j$$$) after some step?
input_spec: The first line contains one integer $$$T$$$ ($$$1 \le T \le 1000$$$) — the number of test cases. Next $$$2T$$$ lines contain test cases — two lines per test case. The first line of each test case contains two integers $$$n$$$ and $$$k$$$ ($$$1 \le n \le 30$$$, $$$2 \le k \le 100$$$) — the size of arrays $$$v$$$ and $$$a$$$ and value $$$k$$$ used in the algorithm. The second line contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$$$ ($$$0 \le a_i \le 10^{16}$$$) — the array you'd like to achieve.
output_spec: For each test case print YES (case insensitive) if you can achieve the array $$$a$$$ after some step or NO (case insensitive) otherwise.

routes.name: Base-k decomposition
routes.description: Represent each element in base k and check that every digit is 0 or 1 and that no power of k is claimed by more than one element.

observations.title: Each power of k is a unique resource
observations.description: At step i, the value k^i can be added to exactly one position or skipped entirely. This means k^0, k^1, k^2, ... are distinct, one-use resources that must be distributed across array positions without reuse.

hints.nudge: What constraint does the step-by-step structure impose on how powers of k are shared among positions?
hints.question: If step i gives you k^i, can two different positions both receive k^i? What does that imply?
hints.formal: Each power k^i is available exactly once globally. It can go to at most one position. So across all elements, no two elements can require the same power of k.

observations.title: Decompose each element in base k
observations.description: Writing a[j] in base k reveals which powers of k are needed to build it. The digit at position i tells you how many copies of k^i are needed for that element. This connects the problem to number representation.

hints.nudge: How can you figure out which powers of k sum to a given number?
hints.question: If you repeatedly divide a[j] by k and look at the remainders, what does each remainder tell you about which powers of k are used?
hints.formal: Compute the base-k representation of each a[j] by repeatedly taking a[j] mod k and dividing by k. The i-th digit tells you how many times k^i appears in the sum for that element.

observations.title: Every digit must be 0 or 1
observations.description: Since each power k^i is available only once, no single element can use k^i more than once. Therefore every digit in the base-k representation of each a[j] must be either 0 or 1. Any digit >= 2 means the answer is NO.

hints.nudge: What happens if some element needs the same power of k twice?
hints.question: When you compute a[j] mod k at some step, what values are acceptable and why?
hints.formal: If any digit in the base-k expansion of a[j] is 2 or more, that element alone would require k^i to be added multiple times, which is impossible. Output NO.

observations.title: No two elements may share the same power
observations.description: Even if every element individually has only 0/1 digits, two elements might both have a 1 in the same digit position, meaning they both need k^i. Since each k^i can go to only one position, any collision across elements means the answer is NO.

hints.nudge: You have checked each element in isolation. Is that sufficient, or is there a global constraint?
hints.question: If a[1] needs k^3 and a[2] also needs k^3, can both be satisfied? How would you detect this?
hints.formal: Maintain a set of used powers. For each element, for each digit position i where the digit is 1, check if power i is already used. If so, output NO. Otherwise mark it as used.

observations.title: Process from lowest power upward
observations.description: Extract digits by repeatedly taking mod k and dividing by k for each element. Process all elements in lockstep from the lowest power upward, checking the two constraints (digit <= 1, no collision) at each level.

hints.nudge: In what order should you process the powers, and how do you extract them efficiently?
hints.question: If you divide each a[j] by k at each step, how do you simultaneously check the collision constraint across elements?
hints.formal: At each power level i: for every element, compute a[j] mod k. If any result >= 2, output NO. Count how many elements have remainder 1; if more than one, output NO. Then set a[j] = a[j] / k for all j. Repeat until all elements are 0.
